reference: GATE CS — Digital Logic / Number Systems

map.condition: Need minimum SOP — group 1s on K-map
map.use: F_{SOP} = \sum (\text{cover all 1s with largest power-of-2 groups})

map.condition: Need minimum POS — group 0s on K-map
map.use: F_{POS} = \prod (\text{cover all 0s with largest power-of-2 groups})

map.condition: Identify prime implicants vs essential prime implicants
map.use: \text{EPI: prime implicant that covers a minterm covered by NO other PI}
\text{Use don't-cares to enlarge groups (SOP) or leave unused (if not helpful)}

formulas.name: K-Map Size vs Variables
formulas.latex: n \text{ variables} \Rightarrow 2^n \text{ cells},\quad \text{rows} \times \text{cols} = 2^n
formulas.plain: n variables => 2^n cells; for 4-var: 4x4 = 16 cells
formulas.whenToUse: Determine the size of the K-map to draw for n input variables.
formulas.trap: Forgetting that axes use Gray code order (00,01,11,10) — NOT binary order; wrong ordering breaks adjacency.

formulas.name: Valid Group (Implicant) Size
formulas.latex: \text{Group size} = 2^k,\quad k \in \{0,1,2,3,\ldots\},\quad \text{group must be rectangular (torus)}
formulas.plain: Group size must be a power of 2: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16. Groups wrap around edges.
formulas.whenToUse: When circling cells on a K-map to form implicants.

terms.means: number of cells in a valid group

formulas.trap: Groups of 3, 5, 6, or 7 are NOT valid. Also, the map wraps — top-bottom and left-right edges are adjacent.

formulas.name: Literals Eliminated by Grouping
formulas.latex: \text{Group of } 2^k \text{ cells eliminates } k \text{ literals from the term}
formulas.plain: Grouping 2^k cells => the resulting product term has (n - k) literals.
formulas.whenToUse: To find how many literals remain after forming a group of size 2^k in an n-variable K-map.

terms.means: literals remaining in the simplified term

formulas.trap: A group of 4 (= 2^2) eliminates 2 literals, leaving n-2 literals — students often forget to subtract from n.

formulas.name: Entered Variable (Variable-Entered) K-Map
formulas.latex: \text{Reduce } n\text{-var map to } (n-1)\text{-var map by entering one variable as cell value}
formulas.plain: One variable is placed as the cell entry (0, 1, A, or A'). Reduces map dimension by 1.
formulas.whenToUse: When K-map cells contain variable expressions instead of just 0/1 — a GATE-favourite compact representation.

terms.means: the entered variable appearing in cells

formulas.trap: Cells containing A and A' can be combined only after expanding back — treat each cell entry as a separate function value, not a constant.

formulas.name: Implicant Definition
formulas.latex: P \text{ is an implicant of } f \iff P=1 \Rightarrow f=1 \quad (\text{all minterms of } P \subseteq \text{ON-set of } f)
formulas.plain: P is an implicant of f if every minterm of P is a minterm of f.
formulas.whenToUse: To verify whether a given product term or literal is an implicant of a function.
formulas.trap: An implicant need NOT be a prime implicant; any single minterm is trivially an implicant.

formulas.name: Prime Implicant (PI) Definition
formulas.latex: \text{PI: an implicant that cannot be combined with any other implicant to form a larger (fewer-literal) implicant}
formulas.plain: A prime implicant is a largest possible group on the K-map that cannot be merged with any adjacent group.
formulas.whenToUse: To identify which groups to consider when writing the minimized expression.
formulas.trap: A term covered by a larger group is NOT a prime implicant — always try to expand every group as much as possible.

formulas.name: Essential Prime Implicant (EPI)
formulas.latex: \text{EPI: a PI that is the ONLY PI covering at least one minterm}
formulas.plain: An EPI must appear in every minimal cover because at least one of its minterms is not covered by any other PI.
formulas.whenToUse: After listing all PIs, to determine which ones are mandatory in the minimum SOP.
formulas.trap: If all minterms of a PI are already covered by EPIs, that PI is redundant and should be excluded to minimize gate count.

formulas.name: Petrick's Method (PI Chart)
formulas.latex: \text{Form PI table: columns = minterms, rows = PIs.}\; P_i = \text{1 if PI}_i \text{ covers minterm}\; j
formulas.plain: Build a prime implicant chart; select minimum set of rows that covers all columns (set-cover problem).
formulas.whenToUse: When multiple PIs can cover remaining minterms and you need to select the minimum subset.

terms.means: rows = PIs, columns = minterms; mark coverage

formulas.trap: Choosing dominated rows/columns first (row domination, column domination) to reduce the table — skipping this step leads to wrong minimum.

formulas.name: Min SOP Procedure via K-Map
formulas.latex: F_{SOP} = \bigcup_{\text{EPIs}} (\text{EPI terms}) + \bigcup_{\text{selected PIs}} (\text{cover remaining 1s})
formulas.plain: Min SOP = (all EPIs) + (minimum extra PIs to cover remaining 1s).
formulas.whenToUse: Deriving the minimum sum-of-products Boolean expression from a truth table or minterm list.

terms.means: Prime Implicant (optional, chosen to minimize literals)

formulas.trap: Don't-care cells can be included in groups to enlarge them (reducing literals) but must NOT appear as standalone terms in the final expression.

formulas.name: Minterm / Sigma Notation
formulas.latex: F(A,B,C,D) = \sum m(i_1, i_2, \ldots) \quad \text{each } i_k \text{ is a row where } F=1
formulas.plain: F = sum_m(0, 1, 2, ...) lists the minterm indices where the function is 1.
formulas.whenToUse: Reading or writing a function in standard minterm (canonical SOP) form before minimization.
formulas.trap: Minterm index is the decimal value of the input pattern; for 4 vars A=MSB, D=LSB unless stated otherwise.

formulas.name: Don't-Care Terms in SOP
formulas.latex: F = \sum m(\text{ON-set}) + \sum d(\text{DC-set}); \quad \text{DC cells}=X\text{ on K-map (use or skip freely)}
formulas.plain: Don't-cares (X) may be included in groups to form larger implicants; they are never required to be covered.
formulas.whenToUse: When the problem provides both minterms and don't-care conditions.
formulas.trap: Including a don't-care as part of a group is allowed; using one as a standalone essential term is wrong — the output is undefined there.

formulas.name: Min POS Procedure via K-Map
formulas.latex: F_{POS}: \text{group the 0s on K-map} \Rightarrow \text{each group gives a sum term (complement variables in group)}
formulas.plain: To get min POS: group the 0-cells (minterms of F'), derive sum terms (OR of literals), multiply them together.
formulas.whenToUse: When the problem asks for minimum Product-of-Sums or when 0s are fewer than 1s (easier to group 0s).

terms.means: complement of F — 0-cells of F become 1-cells of F'

formulas.trap: Variables that are 0 in a POS group appear uncomplemented in the sum term; variables that are 1 appear complemented — opposite of SOP convention.

formulas.name: Maxterm / Pi Notation
formulas.latex: F(A,B,C,D) = \prod M(j_1, j_2, \ldots) \quad \text{each } j_k \text{ is a row where } F=0
formulas.plain: F = prod_M(indices) lists maxterm indices where the function is 0.
formulas.whenToUse: Reading or writing a function in standard maxterm (canonical POS) form.
formulas.trap: Sum of minterms and product of maxterms of the same function use complementary index sets: \(\sum m\) indices + \(\prod M\) indices = all \(2^n\) rows.

formulas.name: SOP–POS Duality via De Morgan
formulas.latex: F_{POS} = \overline{\overline{F}_{SOP}} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \text{minimize } F' \text{ as SOP, then complement entire expression}
formulas.plain: Get F' SOP (group 0s), then apply De Morgan to each term to obtain F POS.
formulas.whenToUse: Quickly converting between SOP and POS forms, or deriving POS from a known SOP.
formulas.trap: When complementing an SOP via De Morgan, OR becomes AND and AND becomes OR, AND every literal is complemented — students often forget to complement literals.

formulas.name: NAND-NAND = SOP
formulas.latex: F = \overline{\overline{A \cdot B} \cdot \overline{C \cdot D}} = AB + CD \quad \text{(two-level NAND = SOP)}
formulas.plain: Two levels of NAND gates implement any SOP expression: first NAND = NOT(product), second NAND = NOT(sum of NOT-products) = sum of products.
formulas.whenToUse: When implementing a minimized Boolean function using only NAND gates.

terms.means: first level: AND inputs; second level: OR the AND results — equivalent to SOP

formulas.trap: Single-literal inputs to the second NAND need an inverter (or extra NAND as NOT); forgetting this increases gate count.

formulas.name: NOR-NOR = POS
formulas.latex: F = \overline{\overline{A + B} + \overline{C + D}} = (A+B)(C+D) \quad \text{(two-level NOR = POS)}
formulas.plain: Two levels of NOR gates implement any POS expression.
formulas.whenToUse: When implementing a minimized POS function using only NOR gates.

terms.means: first level: OR inputs; second level: AND the OR results — equivalent to POS

formulas.trap: For NOR-NOR, start from the minimum POS form, not SOP — using SOP will give wrong results with NOR gates.

formulas.name: XOR from NAND Gates
formulas.latex: A \oplus B = \overline{A \cdot \overline{A \cdot B}} \cdot \overline{B \cdot \overline{A \cdot B}} \quad \Rightarrow \text{minimum 4 NAND gates}
formulas.plain: A 2-input XOR requires a minimum of 4 NAND gates.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked minimum NAND gates to implement XOR (a classic GATE question).
formulas.trap: Attempting a naive NAND conversion of A'B + AB' gives 5 gates; the shared NAND(A,B) gate reduces it to 4.

formulas.name: Minimizing Gates for Restricted Input Sets (BCD)
formulas.latex: \text{Treat inputs } 10\text{–}15 \text{ as don't-cares for BCD circuits} \Rightarrow \text{larger K-map groups possible}
formulas.plain: For BCD (0-9) circuits, minterms 10-15 are invalid and treated as don't-cares, allowing simpler expressions.
formulas.whenToUse: Designing minimal combinational circuits for BCD digits (0-9) or other restricted input domains.

terms.means: Binary Coded Decimal — only minterms 0-9 are valid
minterms 10-15 for BCD; output can be 0 or 1

formulas.trap: Using don't-cares only for SOP groups that expand the 1-cells; do not let them expand groups of 0-cells unless you are minimizing POS.

formulas.name: QM Adjacency Condition
formulas.latex: \text{Two minterms } m_i, m_j \text{ are adjacent} \iff |\text{ones}(i) - \text{ones}(j)| = 1 \text{ AND } i \oplus j = 2^k
formulas.plain: Two minterms can be combined if they differ in exactly one bit position (XOR = power of 2) AND have one-counts differing by 1.
formulas.whenToUse: Applying the Quine-McCluskey tabular method to find prime implicants algorithmically (especially for > 4 variables).

terms.means: number of 1-bits in binary representation of i
XOR of the two minterm indices; must be a power of 2

formulas.trap: Only minterms from adjacent groups (one-count differs by exactly 1) may be paired; comparing across two group-level differences is invalid.

formulas.name: Dash (–) Notation in QM Tables
formulas.latex: m_i \cup m_j = \text{term with dash at differing bit position};\; \text{two groups combine only if dashes are in same positions}
formulas.plain: When two minterms combine, the differing bit is replaced by '-'. Groups in the next round must have dashes in identical positions.
formulas.whenToUse: Tracking combined terms in the QM table during iterative combination rounds.

terms.means: eliminated variable at that bit position

formulas.trap: A term with a dash in position 3 can only combine with another term with a dash in position 3 (AND differing in exactly one other bit).
